Title: Multiple independent losses of the biosynthetic pathway for two tropane alkaloids in the Solanaceae family

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Medicinal HS biosynthesis / tropine formation branch
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream of dedicated HS branch; first HS-specific step from tropinone to tropine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces tropinone to tropine in an NADPH-dependent reaction.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor required for downstream HS biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point; pivotal intermediate-forming enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Medicinal tropane alkaloids used for nerve agent poisoning, Parkinson’s disease, and neuromuscular disorder treatment.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes tropine production and thereby supports hyoscyamine/scopolamine biosynthesis; reduced-activity or inactive alleles in non-HS species limit pathway output.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TRI homologs are conserved across Solanaceae syntenic blocks. 2. VIGS of AtTRI in Anisodus tanguticus reduced tropine accumulation. 3. Recombinant TRI proteins from HS-producing species showed strong tropinone reductase activity, whereas potato StTRI lacked detectable activity and Lycium chinense LcTRI showed much lower catalytic efficiency. 4. Gain-of-function mutations restored activity in StTRI, while reciprocal loss-of-function mutations reduced or abolished BaTRI activity. 5. McTRI from Mandragora caulescens displayed exceptionally high catalytic efficiency, making it a strong engineering candidate.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value engineering target for installing HS biosynthesis in Solanaceae crops; site-directed editing of inactive orthologs plus stacking with missing downstream genes is recommended; McTRI is especially promising because of very high catalytic efficiency.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in roots and leaves of HS-producing species; especially high in secondary roots of A. tanguticus.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots, especially secondary roots in A. tanguticus, for HS accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Lycium chinense; Solanum tuberosum
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Lycium chinense; Solanum tuberosum
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS in A. tanguticus; recombinant enzyme assay; UPLC-MS product verification; kinetic analysis; site-directed gain-of-function and loss-of-function mutagenesis; ancestral sequence analysis; molecular docking
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In A. tanguticus, highest in secondary roots; also expressed in root, leaf, and stem datasets.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: TRV-based VIGS system in A. tanguticus; pET28a bacterial expression system
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing and RNA-seq; BioProject PRJNA765943, PRJNA765960, PRJNA765963, PRJNA903289
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Critical amino acid sites include positions 109, 155, 167, 201, and 243; residues 109 and 167 help form a tropinone-binding hydrophobic pocket. Syntenic TRI blocks are present broadly across Solanaceae, including pseudogenized/incomplete copies in some non-HS species.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses tropine with phenyllactylglucose to form littorine.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms key dedicated intermediate toward hyoscyamine/scopolamine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point; initiates dedicated HS biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes littorine formation and dedicated HS pathway flux.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LS is an essential downstream HS-specific gene with conserved homologs in HS-producing species. 2. VIGS of AtLS in A. tanguticus reduced littorine-related pathway products. 3. AtLS and BaLS showed in vivo activity in tobacco when co-expressed with AbUGT1 and supplied with substrates. 4. In several non-HS species, LS is retained only as an incomplete pseudogene, supporting pathway loss by pseudogenization.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential gene to stack when engineering HS production in non-HS Solanaceae; missing or pseudogenized LS may need heterologous introduction rather than editing alone.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in roots and leaves of HS-producing species; elevated in secondary roots of A. tanguticus.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Atropa belladonna
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Atropa belladonna
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Substrate co-infiltration with tropine and phenyllactate in transient assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS in A. tanguticus; transient co-expression assay in tobacco; LC-MS metabolite detection; microsynteny/pseudogene analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In A. tanguticus, expressed in roots, leaves, stems, with strong expression in secondary roots.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing and RNA-seq; BioProject PRJNA765943, PRJNA765960, PRJNA765963, PRJNA903289
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LS pseudogenization in non-HS species includes exon loss, exemplified by Iochroma cyaneum.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool for phenyllactylglucose and littorine formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies phenyllactate precursor for downstream HS biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPAR homologs from Brugmansia arborea, Mandragora caulescens, and Petunia inflata converted phenylpyruvic acid to phenyllactate in vitro. 2. PPAR is retained and functional even in some non-HS species, indicating partial ancestral pathway retention. 3. Microsynteny around PPAR is less conserved in some species, likely due to translocation/synteny erosion.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful precursor-supply gene for pathway reconstruction; in some crops endogenous orthologs may already be functional and can be leveraged.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in roots and leaves of HS-producing species.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Petunia inflata
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Petunia inflata
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assay with phenylpyruvic acid and NADPH
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein expression; in vitro enzyme assay; LC-MS product detection
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing and RNA-seq; BioProject PRJNA765943, PRJNA765960, PRJNA765963, PRJNA903289
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PPAR is one of the pathway genes retained functionally in non-HS species, supporting random downstream gene loss rather than complete pathway absence.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: P450-mediated rearrangement of littorine to hyoscyamine aldehyde.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for conversion of littorine toward hyoscyamine/scopolamine branch.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP80F1 homologs from HS-producing species were functionally active in transient tobacco assays when co-expressed with HDH. 2. A Petunia inflata CYP80F1 homolog lacked a complete functional domain, consistent with non-HS phenotype. 3. Capsicum species contain collinear CYP80F1 pseudogenes, supporting repeated downstream pathway losses.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important downstream gene to add or repair in non-HS Solanaceae; endogenous pseudogenes in some species may not be repairable without substantial sequence replacement.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in roots and leaves of HS-producing species.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Atropa belladonna; Petunia inflata
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Atropa belladonna; Petunia inflata
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient expression with littorine substrate infiltration
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in tobacco; LC-MS metabolite detection; domain analysis; microsynteny/pseudogene analysis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing and RNA-seq; BioProject PRJNA765943, PRJNA765960, PRJNA765963, PRJNA903289
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Assayed jointly with HDH because direct aldehyde intermediate handling is difficult; non-HS lineages often show pseudogenization.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: downstream step converting hyoscyamine aldehyde to hyoscyamine
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes or converts hyoscyamine aldehyde to hyoscyamine.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product hyoscyamine, precursor to scopolamine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports hyoscyamine production and thereby scopolamine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HDH homologs from HS-producing species and from Convolvulaceae outgroups functioned in coupled tobacco assays with CYP80F1 to produce hyoscyamine from littorine. 2. HDH syntenic blocks were not found in non-HS Solanaceae, but complete homologs were found in Convolvulaceae, implying ancestral origin predating family divergence. 3. VIGS of AtHDH did not reduce hyoscyamine as expected, suggesting HDH may be sufficient but dispensable or genetically complemented in A. tanguticus.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Functional redundancy/compensation may complicate engineering outcomes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Necessary consideration for full HS pathway reconstruction, but possible redundancy suggests additional aldehyde-processing activities may need to be evaluated.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in roots and leaves of HS-producing species.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Atropa belladonna; Ipomoea triloba; Ipomoea nil
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea; Mandragora caulescens; Atropa belladonna; Ipomoea triloba; Ipomoea nil
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transient expression with littorine substrate infiltration
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS in A. tanguticus; transient co-expression assay in tobacco; LC-MS metabolite detection; comparative synteny
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In A. tanguticus, expressed in root/secondary root datasets.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: TRV-based VIGS system; pEAQ-HT transient expression vector
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing and RNA-seq; BioProject PRJNA765943, PRJNA765960, PRJNA765963, PRJNA903289
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors note HDH may be sufficient but dispensable for hyoscyamine biosynthesis due to possible additional unidentified complementary genes.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes a two-step epoxidation/hydroxylation sequence converting hyoscyamine to scopolamine via anisodamine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anisodamine and scopolamine accumulation from hyoscyamine.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VIGS of AtH6H in A. tanguticus reduced anisodamine and scopolamine accumulation. 2. In vitro assays confirmed enzymatic activity of McH6H and LcH6H. 3. Prior studies had already shown H6H activity in A. tanguticus and B. arborea; together these data support ancestral pathway retention and partial functionality in some non-HS species.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Terminal gene required for scopolamine production; useful for upgrading hyoscyamine-producing systems to scopolamine-producing platforms.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in roots and leaves of HS-producing species; elevated in secondary roots of A. tanguticus.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots/secondary roots for scopolamine and anisodamine.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Mandragora caulescens; Lycium chinense; Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Mandragora caulescens; Lycium chinense; Anisodus tanguticus; Brugmansia arborea
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Anisodus tanguticus; Escherichia coli enzyme system
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assay with hyoscyamine or anisodamine as substrate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS in A. tanguticus; recombinant enzyme assay; HPLC/UPLC-MS metabolite detection
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In A. tanguticus, expressed in roots including secondary roots.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: TRV-based VIGS; pET28a bacterial expression system
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing and RNA-seq; BioProject PRJNA765943, PRJNA765960, PRJNA765963, PRJNA903289
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LcH6H remains enzymatically active despite L. chinense being non-HS-producing, indicating random loss of other pathway components rather than universal inactivation.
